title: Smoke on Upholstery
timeEstimate: 30 minutes for light smoke; fire smoke = pro remediation
stainChemistry: Smoke residue is a mix of carbon particulate (soot), tar, oily condensates, and — depending on source — nicotine, creosote, or combustion-byproduct acids. Two very different cases: (1) LIGHT SMOKE (cigarette / incense / one-time kitchen mishap / candle soot) = surface nicotine yellowing + loose soot that responds to careful cleaning, and (2) FIRE SMOKE (house fire, apartment fire, smoke contamination from a neighbor's fire) = heavy saturation of soot + tar + acidic condensates + health-hazard VOCs that have penetrated fiber and batting. The latter is a professional fire-restoration job, not a cleaning job. Attempting to DIY fire-smoke upholstery can cause inhaled health exposure and usually fails anyway because the cushion batting is saturated. On light smoke, the #1 mistake is WIPING with a damp cloth — that drags soot in rather than removing it, leaving a gray smear that's often worse than the original. Vacuum first, always.
whyThisWorks: For light smoke: dry soot removal (vacuum / dry sponge / chemical sponge) pulls loose particulate OUT before any moisture makes it smear. Then solvent (Goo Gone / POG) dissolves tar. Dawn emulsifies. For nicotine yellowing, peroxide oxidizes the pigment. For fire smoke or heavy saturation, there is no home chemistry that reaches cushion batting or neutralizes acidic condensates — certified fire restoration (IICRC S500) is the correct tool. Knowing which case you're in is half the protocol.
defaultAssumption: Home smoker / ex-smoker's furniture for resale = light smoke + nicotine. Incense / candle soot / kitchen mishap = light smoke. House fire / neighbor fire / smoke contamination from nearby structure fire = fire smoke = pro only. The scope check is the whole fork.

spottingProtocol.agent: Fork: Light Smoke vs Fire Smoke
spottingProtocol.instruction: FIRE SMOKE (house fire, apartment fire, smoke from neighbor fire, commercial building fire): STOP. This is fire restoration, not cleaning. Call a licensed fire-restoration company (IICRC S500 certified). DIY risks inhaling VOCs that are health hazards, and the cushion batting is saturated with acidic condensates you can't reach. LIGHT SMOKE (cigarette, incense, candle soot, one-time kitchen): continue below. When in doubt, treat as fire smoke.

Mask + gloves. Find the upholstery care code: W / W-S = full protocol OK. S-only = solvent only, no Dawn rinse. X = vacuum only, pro. Even for "light" smoke, nicotine and tar condensates can irritate skin and airways — PPE is not optional.

spottingProtocol.technique: HEPA vacuum with upholstery attachment, no contact with surface
spottingProtocol.instruction: HEPA vacuum the upholstery with an upholstery attachment. Keep the nozzle just above the surface — don't press it into the fabric, which would grind soot in. Work in overlapping passes across the whole piece, not just the visible dirty area. DO NOT wipe, damp-cloth, or damp-brush first — moisture turns loose soot into a gray smear that's MUCH harder to remove. Dry removal first, always.

A dry chemical sponge (also called a soot sponge or vulcanized rubber sponge) picks up soot mechanically. Wipe in long, straight strokes from clean area toward dirty area. DO NOT wet it. Discard the used sponge in a sealed bag — don't wash and re-use, and don't touch your face with the dirty side.

spottingProtocol.agent: Goo Gone or POG for Tar Residue
spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent tamp from top with cloth
spottingProtocol.equipment: Goo Gone or POG on white cotton cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply to white cloth, tamp the remaining tar / nicotine residue spots from the top. Outside-in. Ventilate. 2–3 passes. Don't pour on upholstery.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dawn + cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: W / W-S code: Dawn + cool water on damp cloth, work with soft brush, blot. S-only: skip — dry buff the solvent residue. Damp-cloth only, never pour.
If yellow nicotine staining remains after solvent + Dawn: hidden-area test, then 3% H₂O₂ on damp cloth 5 min. Blot. Only for nicotine residual — skip for non-smoker smoke.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dry towel + weight, fan, open windows
spottingProtocol.instruction: Weighted dry towel 30 min. Fan the room, open windows. Smoke ODOR is the hardest part — ventilation over days is often needed. Ozone treatment is a pro option for persistent odor (specialized equipment, room cleared of people / pets / plants during use).

steps: 1. Fork: light smoke or fire smoke? Fire = IICRC S500 restoration, not cleaning.
3. HEPA vacuum dry — no damp contact first.
4. Dry chemical sponge for embedded soot.
5. Goo Gone / POG tamp from top for tar residue.
6. Dawn damp cloth (W/W-S) or dry buff (S).
8. Weighted towel + fan-dry + ventilate. Ozone pro option for odor.
warnings: FIRE SMOKE = IICRC S500 restoration, NOT cleaning
DO NOT WIPE WITH DAMP CLOTH before vacuuming — smears soot
PPE required — soot + nicotine + VOCs are respiratory hazards
Non-HEPA vacuum recirculates soot through the room
Odor remediation may need ozone treatment (pro)

homeSolutions.agent: First — Is This Fire Smoke or Light Smoke?
homeSolutions.instruction: This is the most important step. FIRE smoke (from a house fire, apartment fire, or even a neighbor's fire that sent smoke into your space) is NOT a cleaning job. It's fire restoration. The upholstery batting is saturated with acidic condensates that home chemistry can't reach, and the soot/VOCs are actual health hazards. Call a licensed fire restoration company (IICRC S500 certified) — your insurance likely covers this. LIGHT smoke (cigarette smoker's chair, incense, candle soot, one-time kitchen mishap, burned popcorn smell) — continue below. When in doubt, treat as fire smoke and call a pro.

Even "light" smoke residue has nicotine, tar, and VOCs that irritate lungs and skin. Put on an N95 mask (not cloth — actual N95) and disposable gloves before you start. If anyone in the house has asthma or respiratory issues, they should be out of the room during cleaning. Open windows for ventilation.

Tag under a cushion. W / W-S = full protocol including water cleaning. S-only = solvent only, no Dawn + water rinse. X = vacuum only, pro. If the cushion cover zips off and is W-marked, taking it off and machine-washing it cold is easier than treating in place.

homeSolutions.agent: DRY VACUUM FIRST — Do Not Wipe With a Damp Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: This is the #1 mistake people make: they see a sooty couch and grab a damp cloth. DON'T. Wet contact with loose soot smears it into a gray film that's much harder to clean than the original. Vacuum FIRST with a HEPA vacuum (a regular vacuum recirculates fine soot back into the room air — a HEPA filter traps it). Use the upholstery attachment, keep the nozzle just above the fabric, don't press it in. Work in overlapping strips across the whole piece.

homeSolutions.agent: Dry Chemical Sponge (Soot Sponge) for Embedded Soot
homeSolutions.instruction: Get a "chemical sponge" or "soot sponge" — a vulcanized rubber sponge sold for fire cleanup (Amazon, $10). Wipe the upholstery in long straight strokes from a clean area toward a dirty one. DO NOT wet the sponge. It picks up embedded soot mechanically without smearing. As the sponge face gets dirty, slice it off with a knife to expose clean rubber, or replace. Bag used sponges sealed.

homeSolutions.agent: Goo Gone for Tar / Nicotine Residue Spots
homeSolutions.instruction: Spots of tar or heavy nicotine yellow stay after dry removal. Apply Goo Gone (orange bottle, hardware/grocery) to a clean white rag — never pour on upholstery. Tamp the spots from the TOP, flip the rag as it picks up brown/yellow. 2–3 passes. Open windows; Goo Gone is flammable — no flame.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Damp Cloth (W / W-S Code Only)
homeSolutions.instruction: If the code is W or W-S: work a drop of Dawn with cool water into a damp cloth, press on the spots to clear Goo Gone residue, blot. If the code is S-only: skip. Dry buff with a clean cloth instead. Damp-cloth only, no pouring.

For nicotine-yellowed upholstery (ex-smoker furniture), 3% peroxide on a damp cloth can further lift the yellow. Hidden-area test first — peroxide can lighten dyed fabric. Not needed for non-smoker smoke.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia, bleach, or alkaline cleaners.

homeSolutions.instruction: Weighted dry towel 30 min. Fan the room, keep windows open for days. Smoke ODOR takes time to clear — the residual VOCs diffuse out of fabric slowly. Baking soda dusted on the upholstery and left overnight (vacuum off) can help mild residual smell. For stubborn odor, a pro can run an OZONE TREATMENT — people, pets, and plants out of the room during use — which oxidizes the last of the VOCs.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Heavy Smoke or Persistent Odor
homeSolutions.instruction: If the upholstery stays smelly after a full cleaning cycle + ventilation days, the cushion batting is likely contaminated and you need pro extraction or ozone. Fire smoke is ALWAYS pro. Tell them: "Smoke on [W / W-S / S / X] upholstery, source [cigarette / incense / candle / FIRE SMOKE], home-treated with HEPA vacuum + dry chemical sponge + Goo Gone + [Dawn] + [peroxide]. [Persistent odor / residual yellow / fire-restoration needed]."

steps: 1. FIRE SMOKE = PRO. Not a DIY cleaning problem.
2. Code check + N95 + gloves. Open windows.
3. HEPA vacuum DRY — do NOT wipe with damp cloth first.
4. Dry chemical sponge for embedded soot.
5. Goo Gone / POG on rag for tar spots.
6. Dawn damp cloth (W/W-S) or dry buff (S).
7. 3% peroxide for nicotine yellow (optional, hidden test).
8. Fan-dry + days of ventilation + baking soda overnight.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if this is fire smoke (pro-restoration job), if smell persists after full ventilation (pro ozone), or if the piece is leather/silk/wool (different protocol).
warnings: FIRE SMOKE is a pro restoration job — not cleaning
NEVER damp-wipe before vacuuming — smears soot into film
PPE — soot + VOCs are respiratory hazards
Never pour solvent or water on upholstery

neverDo: Never use non-HEPA vacuum — spreads soot
fiberSensitivities: W / W-S upholstery: tolerates full protocol
S-only: solvent only, no water rinse
Leather: different protocol — do not use solvent

materialWarnings: LIGHT smoke vs FIRE smoke is the whole fork
Fire smoke is a restoration job — not cleaning
Dry removal BEFORE wet — don't smear soot

escalation.when: Fire smoke (any source), persistent odor after full ventilation, heavy nicotine saturation (years of smoker occupancy), leather / silk / wool, or any symptom of VOC exposure in the household.
escalation.whatToTell: "Smoke on [W / W-S / S / X] upholstery, source [cigarette / incense / candle / FIRE SMOKE], age [recent / long-term], home-treated with HEPA vacuum + dry chemical sponge + Goo Gone + [Dawn / peroxide]. [Persistent odor / residual yellow / fire restoration needed]."
escalation.specialistType: IICRC S500 fire/smoke restoration specialist (for fire smoke) OR professional upholstery cleaner with ozone capability (for persistent odor)

contextQuestions: LIGHT smoke (cigarette, candle, incense, one-time kitchen) or FIRE smoke (house / apartment / neighbor fire)?
Upholstery code — W, W-S, S, X, unknown?
Long-term smoker occupancy or recent one-time event?
Any respiratory symptoms in the household?
Is this about visible soot, odor, or both?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The fire-vs-light split determines everything. Fire smoke is NEVER a home-cleaning job. Long-term smoker yellowing is the tough subset of light smoke — nicotine saturates cushion batting, so surface treatment helps but may not fully clear. Ozone treatment is the pro option for persistent odor across all light-smoke variants.
